narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 49% chance that you might not pass the bar exam on your first attempt, which is the threshold we use to make these tough admission decisions. You have some strong points in your profile, like an undergraduate GPA of 3.40 that's above the typical 3.21 average, and you're planning to attend full-time, which is the norm for most students. However, other aspects stood out, including your LSAT score of 19—much lower than the average of about 37—your Year 1 school decile ranking of 8 and Year 3 ranking of 9 (both higher and thus indicating lower performance compared to peers' averages around 5.7), and your lower-middle family income bracket. When the model analyzed your situation, it found your Year 3 decile ranking to be the single most influential factor, as it significantly pushed the prediction toward expecting bar exam challenges. Right behind that was your Year 1 decile, which also contributed strongly in the same direction by highlighting early academic struggles relative to classmates. Your family income level ranked third in influence and likewise nudged the outlook toward potential failure, followed closely by your low LSAT score doing the same. Your undergraduate GPA came next, offering a mild counterbalance but not enough to shift the overall prediction, while your Asian racial background had a smaller push toward the riskier outcome. In a positive turn, being female provided a helpful influence toward predicting success, as did your full-time status, though these were among the least impactful factors overall. Compared to typical students—who often have stronger early grades, higher LSATs, higher family incomes, and are more commonly white males—your profile showed more risks in those key academic and background areas that historically correlate with bar passage. The good news is that several of these can be addressed to boost your future odds: consider retaking the LSAT to aim closer to or above the average, focus on building consistently stronger academic performance in any future law studies to improve your decile rankings, and explore scholarships or support programs that might help offset income-related barriers. With targeted preparation like bar exam prep courses emphasizing your weaker areas, students in similar spots have turned things around on reapplication. You're clearly capable, given your solid GPA, so keep pushing forward—we're rooting for your success next time.
